## [00:00 - 03:03]

Hey, what's going on guys? It's Jacob. I wanted to bring you a quick training on something called the story pitch framework. Now this is something that you can use in video, in written. It can be used in organic posts, in paid media. It's a really, really just great framework to have under your belt that you can use at any time to sell something. So like I said, you can do this in your videos, you can do this in your written posts, in anything, and you can use this both across your free platforms, in your Facebook group, on your profile, on YouTube, wherever you want to pitch, or you can even use this framework to write up ads if that's what you're playing with. But it's a go-to. It's one Kern talks about all the time. It's derived from really old school direct response that's sold hundreds of millions of dollars, probably into the billions at this point, over a very, very long period of time. It's tried, it's tested, and I'm all about frameworks. I love having some kind of structure to play to. So I want to keep this simple. There's six parts to it. You can kind of get a little bit creative with it, but I want to arm you with this so that you can start to integrate it into your content and into your sales, because we want it to be clear to our audience that we're making pictures. So let's dive into this. I'm going to talk you through the framework, and then I'm going to give you an example I quickly typed up just for SAA so that you can see how it would kind of look. The story pitch framework. So starting with some kind of cliffhanger headline, something that grabs their attention, something that is kind of going to pique people's curiosity, and they're just going to go, oh, that's interesting. And then your goal is really just to get them into the intro. Then in the intro, you're going to give a little bit of an introduction to yourself, to kind of what you're going to talk about. And really, you want to try and pull out mutual frustrations, mutual things that you're struggling with. That may not be like business struggles, but it could be life struggles. It could be working a job. It could be tech issues, overwhelm, whatever your struggle, whatever struggle your audience is feeling that you've also felt in the past. Try to get that to come across in the intro so that you're immediately pulling out your ideal customers. Then we go into a backstory, which talks about these frustrations and more, and it shares your journey of struggle. So it shares what you went through. It shares the kind of low point that you really struggled with. And the goal here as well is to pull out more of those mutual frustrations so that the person reading it, who is your ideal customer, is going, yep, I can relate. I've been through that. And the journey of struggle reinforces that even more because they'll be able to relate to parts of the story. You probably can't relate in exact detail, but they've probably experienced it. Maybe it's manifested in a different way. Then we go into the discovery slash intro to offer.
